heroOverview: Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis — the coiled tube that lies along the back of each testis and serves as the conduit through which sperm mature and travel toward the vas deferens. It is the most common cause of acute scrotal pain in adult men, accounting for approximately 600,000 clinic visits annually in the United States. In sexually active men under 35, Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are responsible for most cases; in men over 35 or those who engage in insertive anal intercourse, Gram-negative enteric bacteria such as Escherichia coli predominate. The hallmark is a progressively worsening, unilateral scrotal ache, often with localised swelling and overlying skin erythema. Fever, urethral discharge, and urinary symptoms are variable. Treatment with antibiotics typically produces resolution within 1-3 weeks, but the condition must be differentiated promptly from testicular torsion — a surgical emergency — which it can mimic clinically.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 600,000 outpatient visits per year in the United States; estimated incidence of 1 in 1,000 men annually (CDC, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Two distinct peaks: men aged 18-35 (STI-associated) and men aged 40+ (enteric organism-associated, often with underlying BPH or urological instrumentation)
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at presentation approximately 30-35 in STI-associated cases; 50-60 in enteric-organism cases
keyStats.globalCases: One of the most common urological conditions globally; incidence closely tracked with STI rates and urological procedure frequency

definition: Epididymitis (ICD-10: N45.1) is inflammation of the epididymis, typically caused by bacterial infection that reaches the epididymis by ascending from the urethra via the vas deferens. The epididymis is a C-shaped structure running along the posterior border of the testis, divisible into the head (caput), body (corpus), and tail (cauda). Inflammation usually begins in the tail and spreads retrograde toward the head; when the testis itself becomes involved, the condition is termed epididymo-orchitis. The aetiology divides by age and sexual behaviour. In men under 35 who are sexually active, Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common cause, followed by Neisseria gonorrhoeae; Chlamydial epididymitis may be clinically subtle with minimal discharge. In men over 35 or those with an obstructive uropathy, the dominant organisms are coliform Gram-negative bacteria — Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas — reflecting a urinary source of infection. Rare causes include Mycobacterium tuberculosis (in endemic regions), Brucella species, and autoimmune or drug-induced inflammation (amiodarone epididymitis). The condition falls within urology and sexual health medicine; the latter is essential when an STI is identified to ensure partner notification and systemic disease management.

typesClassification.description: Caused by Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae, predominantly in sexually active men under 35; often accompanied by urethritis, discharge, and risk of partner transmission. Requires STI-directed antibiotics and partner notification.

Caused by Gram-negative coliform bacteria from the urinary tract, typically in men over 35 with underlying BPH, urethral stricture, or after urological procedures. Treated with fluoroquinolones or trimethoprim.

Infection has extended beyond the epididymis to involve the testis itself; testicular involvement is suggested by diffuse testicular tenderness and swelling in addition to epididymal thickening. Carries a slightly higher complication risk.

Haematogenous spread of Mycobacterium tuberculosis to the epididymis; presents as chronic, painless scrotal swelling rather than acute pain; often bilateral and associated with a history of tuberculosis exposure. Rare in developed countries.

Amiodarone and its active metabolite accumulate in the epididymis and cause non-infectious inflammatory epididymitis in approximately 3-11% of men on the drug; it resolves with dose reduction and does not require antibiotics.

primarySymptoms: Progressive unilateral scrotal pain, typically starting at the back of the testis and worsening over hours to days — the gradual onset distinguishes epididymitis from the sudden onset of testicular torsion.

Scrotal swelling and tenderness, initially localised to the epididymis at the posterior-superior pole of the testis; the testis itself is tender to pressure but may be distinguishable from the more focally tender epididymis early in the course.
Scrotal skin erythema and warmth overlying the swollen epididymis, reflecting local inflammation.
A palpable, tender, sausage-shaped swelling along the posterior border of the testis — the thickened, inflamed epididymis.
Urethral discharge or dysuria indicating coexistent urethritis, particularly in STI-associated cases; discharge may be clear (Chlamydial) or yellow-green (gonococcal).
Urinary frequency, urgency, and dysuria reflecting associated bladder or urethral inflammation, more prominent in enteric-organism epididymitis.
Fever above 38°C in approximately 30-40% of cases, particularly when the testis is also involved or when the causative organism is Gram-negative.
Groin or lower abdominal ache radiating from the scrotum along the course of the vas deferens toward the inguinal canal.

Prehn's sign — relief of scrotal pain when the scrotum is elevated — is classically positive in epididymitis, in contrast to torsion where elevation worsens pain; however, this sign is unreliable and should not replace imaging.
Dyspareunia or ejaculatory pain in the days preceding the presentation, indicating ascending infection from the urethra through the vas deferens.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild scrotal discomfort at the back of the testis, particularly when there has been a recent episode of urethritis, urinary infection, or new sexual exposure.
A new urethral discharge or dysuria appearing several days before scrotal swelling — the ascending infection sequence.
Low-grade fever accompanying progressive posterior scrotal ache in a man with known BPH or recent cystoscopy.
Scrotal skin that feels warmer than usual on self-examination, prompting same-day medical assessment.
emergencySigns: Sudden, severe scrotal pain at rest — this should be treated as testicular torsion until proven otherwise by urgent ultrasound, as torsion requires surgical exploration within 4-6 hours to save the testis.
Rapid progression to a hard, tender, diffusely swollen hemi-scrotum with skin discoloration, suggesting either torsion or rapidly worsening epididymo-orchitis with vascular compromise.
Fever above 39°C with rigors and systemic signs of sepsis requiring emergency hospital admission for intravenous antibiotics.
A fluctuant area of the scrotum suggesting abscess formation, which requires surgical drainage.

causes.description: The most common cause in sexually active men under 35; the intracellular obligate bacterium infects the urethral mucosa and spreads retrograde along the vas deferens to the epididymis. Urethral symptoms may be minimal or absent, making clinical suspicion essential in sexually active young men with scrotal pain.

The second most common STI-associated cause; gonococcal urethritis produces more pronounced urethral discharge and dysuria than Chlamydia, but both can ascend to cause epididymitis. Co-infection with Chlamydia is common, which is why dual antibiotic treatment is recommended for STI-associated epididymitis.

causes.cause: Gram-negative enteric bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas)
causes.description: In men over 35, coliform organisms from the urinary tract ascend via the prostatic urethra and vas deferens; incomplete bladder emptying from BPH and urinary tract instrumentation increase this risk. These organisms require different antibiotics than STI pathogens.

Cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, urethral catheterisation, and vasectomy can introduce bacteria into the urethra and vas deferens; post-procedural epididymitis represents the commonest urological procedure-related complication, occurring in 0.5-2% of cases without antibiotic prophylaxis.

causes.cause: Bladder outlet obstruction and intraprostatic reflux
causes.description: BPH and urethral stricture force urine into the prostatic ducts under high pressure during voiding (intraprostatic reflux); contaminated urine can then track via the ejaculatory ducts and vas deferens to the epididymis, seeding infection with enteric organisms.

In endemic regions or in immunocompromised patients, Mycobacterium tuberculosis or Brucella species reach the epididymis via the bloodstream; this presents as chronic, painless scrotal swelling rather than acute inflammation and is confirmed by culture or biopsy.

riskFactors.factor: Recent unprotected sexual intercourse or new sexual partner
riskFactors.description: Sexual transmission of Chlamydia and gonorrhoea drives the majority of epididymitis cases in men under 35; consistent condom use and STI screening with new partners are the most effective preventive interventions.

Prostatic enlargement causes high-pressure voiding and intraprostatic reflux of urine into the ejaculatory ducts and vas deferens; men with BPH have approximately 2-3 times the risk of enteric-organism epididymitis compared to men with normal outflow.

Cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, urethral catheterisation, and vasectomy introduce bacteria directly into the lower urinary tract and vas deferens; pre-procedural antibiotic prophylaxis reduces but does not eliminate the risk.
Like BPH, strictures cause high-pressure voiding with intraprostatic reflux; men with strictures have recurrent epididymitis risk until the stricture is treated.
HIV infection, transplant immunosuppression, and diabetes impair the local immune response, allowing organisms that would normally be cleared to establish epididymal infection.

riskFactors.factor: Bladder catheter or indwelling urethral stent
riskFactors.description: Indwelling urethral catheters provide a surface for bacterial ascent directly to the bladder and on to the vas deferens; catheter-associated epididymitis accounts for a small but significant subset, particularly in hospitalised men.

Prior STI increases the likelihood of re-exposure and indicates a pattern of sexual behaviour associated with higher STI incidence; it is also the strongest predictor of Chlamydial epididymitis in case-control studies.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active men aged 18-35 with STI-related epididymitis
Men aged 40 and over with BPH or urethral obstruction
Men who have recently undergone cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, or catheterisation
Diabetic and immunocompromised men of any age
Men who practise insertive anal intercourse (higher risk of Gram-negative organisms)
diagnosisOverview: Epididymitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis supported by imaging and microbiological tests. The history should cover symptom duration and onset (gradual in epididymitis versus sudden in torsion), sexual history including recent partners and condom use, prior STI, urological procedures, urinary symptoms, and medication history (amiodarone). Physical examination seeks a tender, thickened epididymis at the posterior testicular pole, scrotal skin changes, and urethral discharge; a cremasteric reflex should be tested — its absence strongly suggests testicular torsion and mandates urgent surgical referral regardless of other findings. Colour Doppler ultrasound of the scrotum is the key investigation: it demonstrates increased blood flow to the epididymis (hyperaemia) characteristic of inflammation and rules out torsion, which shows absent or reduced flow. Sensitivity of ultrasound for epididymitis exceeds 85-90%. Microbiological workup includes a mid-stream urine culture, first-void urine or urethral swab for Chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT, and in systemic illness, blood cultures. A Gram stain of urethral discharge is a rapid bedside test that identifies gonococci (Gram-negative intracellular diplococci) within minutes. Blood tests — full blood count, CRP — are useful when fever or systemic illness is present. In men over 35, urine flow rate and post-void residual measurement identify underlying obstruction. Tuberculosis should be considered in men with chronic presentation, bilateral disease, or a history of TB exposure; confirmed by AFB culture of urine and epididymal aspirate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates epididymitis (increased blood flow, hyperaemia) from testicular torsion (absent or reduced flow) and identifies complications such as abscess, infarction, or hydrocele; it is the essential imaging investigation for any acute scrotal pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel is applied to the scrotum and a probe is moved gently over the skin; the scan takes 15-20 minutes and is painless. Results are available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine NAAT for Chlamydia and gonorrhoea
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the two most common causative organisms in sexually active men under 35, guides specific antibiotic choice, and determines whether partner notification is required.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A first-void urine sample (the first 15-20 mL of the urinary stream) is used rather than mid-stream; results take 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Gram-negative enteric organisms in non-STI epididymitis, particularly in older men with urinary sources of infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clean mid-stream urine sample is collected and sent for culture; results available in 24-48 hours. Start empirical antibiotics while awaiting results.

diagnosticTests.test: Urethral discharge Gram stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid identification of gonorrhea from a urethral swab; a Gram stain showing Gram-negative intracellular diplococci is diagnostic of gonorrhoea pending culture confirmation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton-tipped swab is passed about 2 cm into the urethral meatus; the swab is briefly uncomfortable and takes seconds to obtain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses severity of systemic infection and fever; blood cultures are drawn before antibiotics when fever above 38.5°C is present to detect bacteraemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws; full results available within 24-48 hours for cultures, within a few hours for inflammatory markers.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine flow rate and post-void residual
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for bladder outlet obstruction in men over 35 presenting with enteric-organism epididymitis, identifying the underlying cause that promotes recurrence.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urinate into a flow meter, then a quick bladder ultrasound measures residual urine; painless and takes about 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for chronic, non-responsive, or atypical presentations to exclude tuberculosis, malignancy, or other granulomatous disease; identifies the organism when non-invasive cultures are negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthetic in an outpatient or day-surgery setting; a needle is passed into the epididymal tissue under ultrasound guidance.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of epididymitis is guided by the most likely causative organism based on age, sexual history, and available microbiology. CDC and EAU guidelines recommend empirical dual antibiotic therapy for sexually active men under 35 with STI-associated epididymitis: a single intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone 500 mg to cover gonorrhoea, combined with doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-14 days for Chlamydia. Where gonorrhoea is not suspected, doxycycline alone for 10-14 days is used for uncomplicated Chlamydial epididymitis. For men likely to have enteric-organism infection — those over 35 or with BPH, after instrumentation, or practising insertive anal intercourse — a fluoroquinolone (ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily for 14 days, or levofloxacin 500 mg once daily for 10 days) is first-line because these agents achieve excellent scrotal tissue penetration and cover the dominant Gram-negative pathogens. All sexual partners of the preceding 60 days must be notified and treated when an STI is identified. Supportive care is central: scrotal elevation with a supportive jockstrap, NSAIDs for pain and swelling, bed rest during the acute phase, and cold packs applied intermittently to reduce oedema. Men who are systemically unwell, have signs of abscess, or have comorbidities requiring intravenous antibiotics should be hospitalised. Epididymal abscess requires surgical exploration and drainage or orchidectomy when the testis is also necrotic. Underlying causes such as BPH should be addressed to prevent recurrence after infection resolves.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose
medicalTreatments.description: Single intramuscular injection of ceftriaxone, given at the initial assessment, covers gonococcal epididymitis; it is combined with doxycycline for dual STI coverage when gonorrhoea cannot be excluded in sexually active men under 35.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose ceftriaxone achieves microbiological cure of gonorrhoea in approximately 98-99% of cases when the organism is susceptible to third-generation cephalosporins.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-14 days
medicalTreatments.description: Standard antibiotic treatment for Chlamydial epididymitis and the Chlamydial component in dual-therapy for STI-associated cases; tetracycline-class with good intracellular penetration. Taken with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Microbiological cure of Chlamydial infection in approximately 95-98% of cases at standard dosing; treatment of partners simultaneously is required to prevent re-infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Levofloxacin 500 mg once daily for 10-14 days
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for enteric-organism epididymitis in men over 35 or those with a urinary source of infection; fluoroquinolones achieve high tissue concentrations in the scrotal contents. Ofloxacin 200 mg twice daily is an alternative with equivalent efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of approximately 85-95% for Gram-negative epididymitis when the organism is susceptible; avoid in regions with high fluoroquinolone resistance rates and in Chlamydial cases where doxycycline is superior.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs reduce scrotal oedema, pain, and fever during the acute phase; used as regular analgesia for the first 1-2 weeks of treatment alongside antibiotics, not as required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically effective for symptomatic relief within 24-48 hours; no direct antibacterial action but reduces time to functional recovery in studies comparing antibiotic-only versus antibiotic-plus-NSAID regimens.

medicalTreatments.name: Scrotal elevation and supportive underwear
medicalTreatments.description: Wearing a supportive jockstrap or athletic supporter elevates the scrotum, improving venous drainage and reducing dependent oedema; simple and highly effective for symptomatic relief. Applied from the first day of treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data consistently show faster reduction in scrotal swelling and pain with scrotal elevation compared to no support; it is universally recommended as a zero-risk adjunct.

surgicalOptions.name: Epididymal abscess drainage or epididymectomy
surgicalOptions.description: When epididymitis progresses to abscess formation — a pus collection within the epididymis — surgical exploration, incision and drainage, or partial epididymectomy is performed under general anaesthesia. If the testis is also necrotic, orchiectomy may be required.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical drainage is curative in approximately 90-95% of epididymal abscesses when performed before testicular compromise; orchiectomy rates in this scenario are approximately 5-20% depending on disease severity.

surgicalOptions.name: Exploration to exclude testicular torsion
surgicalOptions.description: When clinical or ultrasound findings do not clearly exclude torsion, surgical scrotal exploration is performed within 4-6 hours; if torsion is found the testis is de-twisted and fixed; if epididymitis is confirmed the exploration itself is both diagnostic and therapeutic (the epididymis is inspected and bilateral orchidopexy is performed).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Testicular salvage rate approaches 95-100% when exploration occurs within 4-6 hours; falls to below 50% after 12 hours.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cold packs and sitz baths
alternativeTreatments.description: Intermittent application of cold packs wrapped in cloth to the scrotum for 20 minutes several times daily reduces oedema and provides analgesia in the first 48-72 hours; warm sitz baths can relieve discomfort after the acute swelling begins to subside.

alternativeTreatments.name: Abstinence from sexual activity during treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: Avoiding intercourse until the antibiotic course is complete and a test-of-cure has been performed prevents re-infection and transmission to partners; not a treatment but an essential behavioural adjunct that maximises cure rate and prevents STI spread.

whySeeSpecialist: A urologist should be consulted for any man with acute scrotal pain to exclude testicular torsion, evaluate for structural causes of recurrent epididymitis, and plan treatment of predisposing conditions such as BPH or urethral stricture. A sexual health physician is essential when an STI is identified, to ensure appropriate partner notification, systemic STI screening, and HIV testing. Men with recurrent epididymitis, bilateral disease, or suspected tuberculous or malignant aetiology require specialist investigation without delay.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a urologist with access to scrotal Doppler ultrasound on the same day, as this is required urgently to exclude torsion. For STI-associated cases, a sexual health or genitourinary medicine clinic is appropriate as the primary or co-managing service, offering partner notification and broader STI assessment. For recurrent or atypical cases, a urologist with andrology or male fertility experience is valuable. Ask about the availability of same-day ultrasound — this is non-negotiable in the acute setting.
preventionStrategies: Use condoms consistently with new or casual sexual partners to prevent Chlamydial and gonococcal epididymitis, the most common causes in men under 35.
Get regular STI screening if sexually active with multiple partners — Chlamydia is frequently asymptomatic and a diagnosis before epididymitis develops prevents the complication.
Treat BPH or urethral stricture to eliminate the bladder outlet obstruction that drives intraprostatic reflux and enteric-organism epididymitis.
Receive antibiotic prophylaxis before cystoscopy, prostate biopsy, or other urological procedures — this is standard of care and should be confirmed with the treating urologist.
Seek same-day assessment for any new scrotal pain — early treatment prevents abscess formation and testicular damage.
lifestyleModifications: Wear a scrotal support (jockstrap) continuously during waking hours for the first 1-2 weeks of treatment to reduce oedema and pain.
Avoid sexual intercourse and masturbation until antibiotic treatment is complete and any STI test-of-cure has been confirmed.
Rest and avoid vigorous physical activity, particularly activities placing pressure on the scrotum (cycling, horse riding), until swelling and pain resolve.
Apply cold packs to the scrotum for 20 minutes every few hours during the acute phase — wrap in cloth, never apply directly to skin.
Ensure sexual partners from the preceding 60 days are informed and tested if an STI was identified — re-infection is the main cause of recurrence in STI-associated cases.
Complete the full antibiotic course even when symptoms improve — stopping early is the most common cause of relapse.

recommended: Adequate hydration — 2 litres of water daily to maintain dilute urine and reduce lower urinary tract irritation
Anti-inflammatory diet (oily fish, nuts, berries, leafy greens) during recovery to support resolution of scrotal inflammation
Probiotic yoghurt or supplements during antibiotic courses to reduce gastrointestinal side effects of doxycycline
Balanced meals with lean protein to support immune function and tissue recovery
avoid: Alcohol during treatment — it increases dehydration and may interact with doxycycline causing nausea; also impairs immune response
Caffeine and bladder irritants during the acute phase if urinary symptoms are present
High-fat, high-sugar foods that worsen glycaemic control in diabetic men prone to recurrent infection

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest from strenuous exercise and activities placing direct pressure on the scrotum (cycling, rowing) is advised during the acute phase of 1-2 weeks. Walking and light activity may be resumed once fever and acute pain resolve, typically within 3-5 days of starting effective antibiotics. Full return to sport and sexual activity should wait until the antibiotic course is completed and all symptoms have resolved. After scrotal surgery, a 6-week recovery with avoidance of lifting and contact sport is standard.
prognosis: With appropriate antibiotic treatment, the prognosis for acute epididymitis is excellent: the vast majority of men recover fully within 1-3 weeks. Scrotal swelling takes longer to resolve than pain — tenderness typically settles within 3-7 days of treatment but induration of the epididymis can persist for 4-8 weeks. Complications affecting fertility — epididymal obstruction from fibrosis — are uncommon but may occur after severe or bilateral disease; approximately 6-10% of men with bilateral epididymitis experience subfertility from ductal obstruction. Recurrence is common when predisposing causes (STI re-exposure, BPH) are not addressed. Testicular atrophy can follow severe epididymo-orchitis with vascular compromise, but this is rare when treatment is started promptly. Men with STI-associated epididymitis who are treated adequately have cure rates above 95% and can expect full return of function.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 24-48 hours of starting effective antibiotics. Scrotal tenderness and dysuria improve within 3-7 days. Swelling and induration of the epididymis take longer — most men notice significant improvement by 2 weeks but residual firmness can persist for 4-8 weeks. The full antibiotic course (10-14 days for STI; 10-14 days for enteric organisms) must be completed. Test-of-cure urine or urethral NAAT is performed 3-4 weeks after completing treatment for STI-associated cases. Return to full normal activity including sport and sexual intercourse is typically possible at 2-4 weeks.
complications: Epididymal abscess — a pus collection within the epididymis requiring surgical drainage; suggested by failure to respond to 72 hours of antibiotics, increasing scrotal swelling, and fluctuance on examination.
Orchitis and epididymo-orchitis — infection extending to the testis, producing more severe pain and fever; carries a higher risk of testicular atrophy from ischaemia and inflammation.

Testicular infarction or atrophy — vascular compromise from severe epididymo-orchitis or an occult torsion diagnosed late causes necrosis and subsequent testicular atrophy with hormone and semen impact.
Chronic epididymitis — persistent or recurrent scrotal pain lasting more than 3 months after infection is cleared; managed with NSAIDs, nerve blocks, or rarely epididymectomy.

Infertility from epididymal obstruction — fibrotic scarring of the epididymal lumen after severe or bilateral disease blocks sperm transit; bilateral epididymitis is a recognised cause of obstructive azoospermia.
Fournierʼs gangrene — extremely rare necrotising fasciitis of the scrotal and perineal tissue arising from overwhelming infection; life-threatening and requires emergency debridement.

supportResources.description: UK NHS plain-English patient information on epididymitis including symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and when to seek urgent care.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Scrotal Pain and Epididymitis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources on urological conditions including epididymitis from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

sources.title: CDC Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines 2021 — Epididymitis
Trojian TH, Lishnak TS, Heiman D. Epididymitis and orchitis: an overview. American Family Physician, 2009
Pilatz A et al. Epididymitis in adults: updated CDC-compliant review of aetiology, diagnosis and treatment. Asian Journal of Andrology, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Epididymo-orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epididymo-orchitis means both the epididymis and testis are inflamed, presenting with diffuse testicular swelling and pain rather than focally posterior tenderness; it is diagnosed on examination and ultrasound and indicates more advanced infection requiring equivalent antibiotic treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated orchitis means only the testis is inflamed, most often from mumps virus in unvaccinated adolescents or young adults; it presents with diffuse painful testicular swelling without the characteristic posterior epididymal tenderness of bacterial epididymitis and does not require antibiotics unless bacterial co-infection is present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Prostatocystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Prostatocystitis presents with perineal pressure, rectal discomfort, urinary symptoms, and a tender boggy prostate on rectal examination without primary scrotal swelling; epididymitis is distinguished by the scrotal finding of a tender posterior epididymal mass.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymo-orchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymo-orchitis is the natural extension of epididymitis when infection spreads to the testis; the same antibiotics are used but with closer monitoring for testicular complications.

relatedConditions.name: Orchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Orchitis and epididymitis share the common presentation of scrotal pain and swelling and must be distinguished clinically and on ultrasound; they sometimes coexist.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Gonorrhoea is the second most common STI cause of epididymitis; all men diagnosed with epididymitis should be screened for gonorrhoea and treated presumptively.

relatedConditions.name: Prostatocystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymitis and prostatocystitis can coexist as part of the same ascending infection; both conditions should be considered in men with concurrent urinary and scrotal symptoms.

faqs.question: What is epididymitis?
faqs.answer: Epididymitis is inflammation of the epididymis — the tube that runs along the back of each testicle where sperm mature. It causes scrotal pain and swelling, usually on one side, and is most often caused by a bacterial infection spreading from the urethra or bladder.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of epididymitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are a dull ache or pain at the back of one testicle that worsens gradually over hours to days, scrotal swelling and tenderness, warmth of the overlying skin, and sometimes fever. Urethral discharge and burning urination occur when there is also a sexually transmitted infection.

faqs.question: What causes epididymitis?
faqs.answer: In sexually active men under 35, the most common causes are Chlamydia trachomatis and gonorrhoea spreading from the urethra. In men over 35, coliform bacteria from the urinary tract — E. coli being the most common — are more often responsible, usually in the context of an enlarged prostate or recent urological procedure.

faqs.question: How is epididymitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is antibiotics, chosen based on the likely cause. For STI-associated epididymitis, a single injection of ceftriaxone plus 10-14 days of doxycycline covers both gonorrhoea and Chlamydia. For urinary-source epididymitis in older men, a fluoroquinolone such as levofloxacin for 10-14 days is used. Scrotal support, rest, and ibuprofen relieve discomfort alongside the antibiotics.

faqs.question: How is epididymitis different from testicular torsion?
faqs.answer: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency where the testis twists on its blood supply, causing sudden, severe pain usually at rest. Epididymitis comes on gradually over hours to days. Both cause scrotal pain and swelling. Any man with sudden severe scrotal pain must have an urgent ultrasound or surgical exploration to rule out torsion — waiting is not safe.

faqs.question: How long does epididymitis take to get better?
faqs.answer: Most men notice significant improvement in pain within 3-7 days of starting effective antibiotics. Swelling and firmness of the epididymis take longer — typically 2-4 weeks. The full antibiotic course must be completed even when symptoms improve. Residual mild firmness for up to 8 weeks after infection is normal.

faqs.question: Is epididymitis an STI?
faqs.answer: In men under 35, epididymitis is usually caused by a sexually transmitted infection — most commonly Chlamydia. If an STI is identified, sexual partners from the preceding 60 days need to be informed and tested. However, epididymitis itself is not directly transmitted through sex — it arises when an STI spreads from the urethra into the epididymis.

faqs.question: Can epididymitis affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Most men with a single episode of epididymitis recover full fertility. Bilateral epididymitis — affecting both sides — or severe disease with epididymal scarring can block sperm transport and cause obstructive azoospermia. If you have had bilateral disease or are concerned about fertility, ask your urologist about a semen analysis.

faqs.question: Does epididymitis require surgery?
faqs.answer: Most cases resolve with antibiotics alone. Surgery is needed only when an abscess forms inside the epididymis, when the testis is also damaged, or when scrotal exploration is required to exclude testicular torsion. Epididymectomy — surgical removal of the epididymis — is occasionally needed for chronic epididymitis that does not respond to medical management.

faqs.question: Is epididymitis the same as epididymo-orchitis?
faqs.answer: Epididymitis means only the epididymis is inflamed. Epididymo-orchitis means both the epididymis and the testicle are inflamed. If the testis becomes involved you will notice diffuse, generalised testicular tenderness rather than pain focused at the back of the testicle. The treatment is the same but complications such as testicular atrophy are slightly more likely.

faqs.question: Do I need to tell my sexual partners if I have epididymitis?
faqs.answer: If an STI — Chlamydia or gonorrhoea — is identified, yes: sexual partners from the preceding 60 days should be notified and tested. They may have no symptoms but can still carry the infection. A sexual health clinic can help with confidential partner notification. This step is essential to prevent re-infection and to protect partners.

faqs.question: Can epididymitis come back?
faqs.answer: It can recur, particularly if the underlying cause is not treated. STI-associated epididymitis recurs with re-infection from an untreated partner. Enteric-organism epididymitis recurs in men with untreated BPH or stricture. Treating the predisposing cause — and consistent condom use for STI prevention — reduces recurrence substantially.

faqs.question: What should I avoid doing when I have epididymitis?
faqs.answer: Avoid sexual intercourse until antibiotics are complete and any STI has been confirmed cleared. Avoid vigorous exercise, cycling, and activities placing pressure on the scrotum. Avoid alcohol during doxycycline treatment as it causes nausea. Do not stop antibiotics early even if symptoms improve — incomplete treatment leads to relapse.

faqs.question: Can epididymitis cause a fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 30-40% of men with epididymitis develop a fever above 38°C, particularly when the testis is also involved or when the infecting organism is Gram-negative. A very high fever above 39°C with rigors, or failure to improve within 48 hours of antibiotics, requires urgent review for complications such as abscess or sepsis.

faqs.question: Can I still have sex with epididymitis?
faqs.answer: No — not until antibiotic treatment is fully completed and any STI test-of-cure confirms clearance. Having sex during treatment risks re-infecting yourself from an untreated partner and delays recovery. It is also uncomfortable. Once fully treated and cleared, there are no restrictions on sexual activity.

faqs.question: What is the ultrasound test for epididymitis?
faqs.answer: A scrotal Doppler ultrasound checks blood flow in the scrotum. In epididymitis, blood flow to the epididymis is increased (hyperaemia). In testicular torsion — which must be excluded urgently — blood flow is absent. The ultrasound is painless, takes 15-20 minutes, and is the essential investigation for any acute scrotal pain.

faqs.question: Is epididymitis more common in older or younger men?
faqs.answer: Both age groups are affected but for different reasons. Men under 35 develop it mainly from STIs (Chlamydia, gonorrhoea). Men over 35 develop it mainly from urinary bacteria associated with prostate enlargement, urethral stricture, or bladder procedures. Recognising this age-based split guides the choice of antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can epididymitis resolve without antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Bacterial epididymitis does not reliably resolve without antibiotics. Waiting risks abscess formation, testicular damage, spread to the prostate or bladder, and systemic infection. Unlike some minor infections, the scrotal location and the risk of fertility damage make early antibiotic treatment non-negotiable rather than a watchful-waiting option.

faqs.question: Does mumps cause epididymitis?
faqs.answer: Mumps virus primarily causes orchitis — inflammation of the testis — rather than isolated epididymitis, and it does so in approximately 30% of post-pubertal men who contract mumps. Widespread MMR vaccination has made mumps orchitis rare. If viral orchitis is suspected alongside epididymitis, viral serological testing is performed and treatment is supportive rather than antibacterial.

metaDescription: Epididymitis causes scrotal pain and swelling from bacterial infection of the epididymis. Learn symptoms, antibiotic treatment, and STI links. Doctor-reviewed.
